title: HTTP/HTTPS: The Web's Language 🌍
description: Understanding the conversation between your browser and the server.

slides.title: HyperText Transfer Protocol 📄
slides.content:
# The Foundation of the Web

Every time you load a webpage, your browser sends an **HTTP Request** to a server, and the server sends back an **HTTP Response**.

**It's a Stateless Protocol**: The server doesn't remember you from one request to the next. (This is why we have Cookies and Sessions).

**Structure of a Request:**
- **Method**: What do you want to do? (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE).
- **Path**: The file or resource location (`/images/logo.png`).
- **Headers**: Metadata about you (Browser type, Cookies, Accepted language).
- **Body**: Data you are sending (usually in POST requests).

quizData.question: You are logging into a website with a username and password. Which HTTP method is almost certainly being used?
quizData.explanation: POST is used to send data securely in the request body. GET would put the password in the URL (bad!).

slides.title: Status Codes: The Server's Reply 🚦
slides.content:
# Memorize These Ranges

Every response comes with a 3-digit code.

- **2xx (Success)**: Everything worked. `200 OK`.
- **3xx (Redirection)**: Go somewhere else. `301 Moved Permanently`, `302 Found`.
- **4xx (Client Error)**: **You** messed up. 
    - `400 Bad Request`
    - `403 Forbidden` (You aren't allowed to see this).
    - `404 Not Found` (The classic broken link).
- **5xx (Server Error)**: **They** messed up. `500 Internal Server Error`.

**Hacker Insight**: A `403 Forbidden` is a challenge! It means there's a restricted area. Try changing the User-Agent header or accessing it from a different IP.

slides.title: HTTPS: The 'S' is for Security 🔒
slides.content:
# HTTP + SSL/TLS Encryption

HTTP sends data in **plain text**. Anyone on the same Wi-Fi network can read your passwords and credit card numbers using a tool like **Wireshark**.

**HTTPS** wraps HTTP inside an encrypted tunnel using **SSL/TLS**.
- **Port**: 443 (instead of 80).
- **How it Works**: The server provides a **Certificate** proving its identity.

**Warning**: If you see a warning about an 'Invalid Certificate', **stop**. Someone might be intercepting your traffic (MITM attack).

slides.title: Seeing HTTP with curl 📡
slides.content: Use `curl` to see the raw HTTP headers and response. This is essential for web hacking.
slides.codeSnippet:
# Basic GET request showing headers
curl -I https://example.com

# See full verbose output
curl -v https://example.com

slides.title: Pro Tip: User-Agent Spoofing 🚀
slides.content:
The **User-Agent** header tells the server what browser you are using. Some servers block certain tools or mobile browsers.

You can change it with curl:
```bash
curl -A "Mozilla/5.0 (iPhone; CPU iPhone OS 14_0 like Mac OS X)" https://example.com
```
This makes your request look like it's coming from an iPhone.
